papers.title: Effects of annealing temperature on microstructure and mechanical properties of a low density δ-TRIP steel plate via intercritical quenching-annealing process
papers.paper_scope: Investigates effects of annealing temperature on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe-8.9Mn-4.6Al-0.29C low density δ-TRIP steel processed by intercritical quenching and annealing.

alloys.alloys_notes: Actual composition from Table 1; nominal composition Fe-9Mn-5Al-0.3C used as alias.

processes.description: Hot rolled from 80 mm to 12 mm in eight passes after reheating at 1100 °C for 2 h, initial rolling temperature ~1050 °C, finishing rolling temperature ~950 °C, then air cooled to room temperature.
Reheated to 1050 °C (ferrite+austenite dual-phase region) for 1 h, followed by water quenching.
After intercritical quenching, annealed at 620 °C for 1 h, then air cooled.
After intercritical quenching, annealed at 680 °C for 1 h, then air cooled.
After intercritical quenching, annealed at 740 °C for 1 h, then air cooled.
After intercritical quenching, annealed at 800 °C for 1 h, then air cooled.

processing_steps.method: Ingot heated to 1100 °C for 2 h, forged to 80 mm × 70 mm
Hot rolling in eight passes (same as HR)
Reheated to 1050 °C for 1 h, water quenching
Hot rolling in eight passes (same as HR)
Reheated to 1050 °C for 1 h, water quenching
Annealed at 620 °C for 1 h, air cooled
Hot rolling in eight passes (same as HR)
Reheated to 1050 °C for 1 h, water quenching
Annealed at 680 °C for 1 h, air cooled
Hot rolling in eight passes (same as HR)
Reheated to 1050 °C for 1 h, water quenching
Annealed at 740 °C for 1 h, air cooled
Hot rolling in eight passes (same as HR)
Reheated to 1050 °C for 1 h, water quenching
Annealed at 800 °C for 1 h, air cooled

structures.overall_structure: Micro-laminated microstructure consisting of δ-ferrite bands and lath martensite/α-ferrite regions, with retained austenite in martensite bands.
Micro-laminated microstructure with δ-ferrite bands thinned/segmented by prior austenite grains, martensite region enlarged, retained austenite less than HR.
Micro-laminated with δ-ferrite band and tempered martensite lamellar; carbides precipitated in tempered martensite; fine reverted austenite grains at lath boundaries.
Micro-laminated with δ-ferrite band and tempered martensite lamellar; fine reverted austenite grains, no carbides.
Micro-laminated with δ-ferrite band and tempered martensite lamellar; highest austenite volume fraction, no carbides.
Micro-laminated with δ-ferrite band and tempered martensite lamellar; slightly lower austenite fraction than QA-740 due to martensitic transformation during cooling; some fresh martensite present.

defect_interaction.quality_description: Interface between δ-ferrite and martensite leads to stress concentration and crack initiation; second cracks observed along interfaces.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Difference in hardness between δ-ferrite and martensite causes strain partitioning; cracks propagate into δ-ferrite band.

defect_interaction.quality_description: Crack initiates at δ-ferrite/tempered martensite interface, propagates into δ-ferrite band forming cleavage steps and river patterns; second cracks and laminar tears observed.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Hardness difference persists; strain-induced martensitic transformation causes volume expansion promoting crack closure.

service_conditions.environment: intended for high-strength automobile steel plates

characterization_methods.key_findings: Laminated microstructure with δ-ferrite bands and lath martensite; retained austenite present.

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase distribution, grain orientation, GND density
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite volume fraction ~16%; high GND density.
δ-ferrite bands thinned/segmented; larger martensite region; less retained austenite.
Austenite ~7%; GND lower than HR but still high.
Carbides (cementite) precipitated in tempered martensite; fine reverted austenite grains.

characterization_methods.purpose: Austenite fraction, KAM maps, grain boundary density
characterization_methods.key_findings: Austenite ~28% (EBSD); GND decreased.
Austenite ~33%; SFE ~36.50 mJ/m².
No carbides; fine austenite grains in tempered martensite.
Austenite ~35% (EBSD).
Austenite ~37%; SFE ~38.27 mJ/m².
Highest austenite fraction; no carbides.
Austenite ~50% (EBSD).
Austenite ~51%; SFE ~33.29 mJ/m².
Austenite fraction slightly lower than QA-740; fresh martensite observed from austenite transformation during cooling.
Austenite ~49% (EBSD).
Austenite ~48%; SFE ~31.53 mJ/m².

characterization_methods.technique: Tensile test (WDW-100E, strain rate 0.375 min⁻¹, room temperature, specimen M10×60 mm, gauge 25 mm)
characterization_methods.key_findings: HR: YS 305 MPa, UTS 1107 MPa, TE 5.6%.
IQ-1050: YS 575 MPa, UTS 929 MPa, TE 2.0%.
QA-620: YS 614 MPa, UTS 864 MPa, TE 44%.
QA-680: YS 542 MPa, UTS 735 MPa, TE 45.6%.
QA-740: YS 498 MPa, UTS 725 MPa, TE 62.4%.
QA-800: YS 469 MPa, UTS 719 MPa, TE 50.4%.
Mixed fracture with cleavage, quasi-cleavage, dimples; second cracks and laminar tears; river patterns and cleavage steps.

analytical_model.model_note: Calculated for BCC and FCC phases; results in Fig. 8(a).

analytical_model.parameters: a0 lattice constant, SFP stacking fault probability, ε50 root mean square microstrain
analytical_model.model_note: Used to estimate SFE of austenite; results in Table 4.

analytical_model.parameters: V_0, V_ε austenite volume fraction before/after test, ε true strain

unmapped_findings: The steel density is 7.34 g/cm³ measured by Archimedes' principle, 6.7% lower than low-carbon low-alloyed steels.
Product of strength and elongation (PSE): HR 6.2 GPa%, IQ-1050 1.9 GPa%, QA-620 38.0 GPa%, QA-680 33.5 GPa%, QA-740 45.2 GPa%, QA-800 36.2 GPa%.
QA-680 exhibited necking at about 25% strain, but then a second stage of uniform elongation until failure due to delayed TRIP effect.

Work hardening rate curves show three stages: stage I sharp drop due to soft phase deformation; stage II slow decrease dominated by dislocation glide; stage III increase due to TRIP effect dominating with possible TWIP contribution.

Grain boundary densities (length per area) from Fig. 8(b): HR - AG boundaries ~0.8, high-angle packet ~0.35, block ~0.75; IQ-1050 - AG ~0.6, block ~1.1; QA-620 - AG ~0.85, block ~0.4; QA-680 - AG ~1.25, block ~0.35; QA-740 - AG ~1.6, block ~0.25; QA-800 - AG ~1.25, block ~0.25. (units arbitrary from figure).
Austenite grain size statistics from EBSD: ~90% of grains <4.8 µm in all QA specimens; fine grains have higher enrichment of alloying elements and higher stability.
The A1 temperature (cementite precipitation) is around 617 °C, which influenced the selection of annealing temperatures.
The peritectic reaction δ-F + L → A occurs at about 1390 °C; maximum austenite fraction ~91.4% at ~970 °C in equilibrium.
Fig. 9(a) XRD profiles before and after tensile test show peaks; the text describes intensity changes but no exact values.
Fractures: HR and IQ-1050 were not analyzed; QA specimens all show laminar tears, second cracks, and mixed cleavage/dimple features.
The steel is considered a typical third generation advanced high strength steel and can substitute TRIP 590T/400Y.
